title: Plan the Launch
developmental_frame: This is the year's capstone, and Think leads it: a real mini-launch to genuine customers. At 11–12 a child can plan it using evidence from their own research (Q3), make reasoned decisions (Q2), and break a real project into ordered steps. Grounding a launch in what they actually found out about customers — rather than hope — is a major, real-world milestone. Keep the scope small, safe, and adult-supported throughout.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is being the strategist of a real launch — using evidence, not wishful thinking, to decide what to sell, to whom, where, and how. It's every thinking skill from two years pointed at one genuine question: how do we make this actually work?

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child owns the plan for a real launch that real customers will attend or buy from. Being genuinely in charge of a strategy — one grounded in their own research and judged by real results — is deeply motivating at this age. The launch is real, the responsibility is theirs, and that pulls out serious, evidence-based thinking.

artifact: A launch plan grounded in research: what to launch, the target customer (from real data), where and when, an ordered step plan, and a clear measurable goal (e.g., 10 real customers or RM50 profit).
success_criteria: The choice of what to launch is justified by research, not just a hunch.
There is a specific target customer and a measurable goal.
The plan has ordered steps and a set launch date agreed with an adult.

lessons.title: Decide what to launch
lessons.concept: Use your research to choose what to launch. The whole point of Q3 was evidence about what people want — now you spend it. Pick the thing your data says real customers will actually pay for, not just the thing you feel like making.
lessons.real_example: Your research found most classmates want spicy snacks and would pay RM2. So you launch a spicy-snack stall — a choice backed by evidence, far safer than guessing they'd want something.
lessons.apply_task: Using your research findings, decide what you will launch. Write one sentence linking your choice to the evidence.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Is your choice driven by your data or by what you personally fancy making? Let the evidence win.
lessons.feedback_task: Explain your choice and its evidence to a parent — does the launch idea follow from your research?
lessons.teach_back_task: Show someone how you used real research to pick what to launch.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This decision rests on your real data about your real community — yours to make. AI can't know what your customers said; only your research can.
success_criteria: A launch idea is chosen and justified by research.

lessons.title: Who's it for, and what's the goal?
lessons.concept: Name your target customer (from your research) and set one measurable goal for the launch — a number you can check afterward. A goal turns a vague hope into something you can actually hit or miss and learn from.
lessons.real_example: Target: kids in my year who buy snacks at break. Goal: sell to 10 real customers and make RM20 profit on launch day. Now success is a clear number, not a feeling.
lessons.apply_task: Write your target customer and one measurable goal for the launch (customers, sales, or profit).
lessons.reflection_prompt: Is your goal ambitious but reachable? Too easy teaches nothing; impossible just disheartens.
lessons.feedback_task: Check your goal with a parent or mentor — is it realistic for a first launch?
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to someone why a measurable goal beats 'I hope it goes well'.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Your target customer comes from your research; your goal is your judgement about what's reachable. Both are yours — set them honestly.
success_criteria: A specific target customer and a measurable goal are set.

lessons.title: Where, when, and how
lessons.concept: Decide the launch setting — a safe pop-up (a school fair, a community table) or a small, adult-supervised online drop. Every launch needs a real place and time, and at your age it needs an adult in the loop for safety.
lessons.real_example: You and a parent decide: a stall at the school open day next Saturday. Real place, real date, adult present. That's a launch that can actually happen safely.
lessons.apply_task: With an adult, choose a safe launch setting, place, and date. Write them into your plan.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Is your setting somewhere your target customer will actually be? Right product, wrong place still fails.
lessons.feedback_task: Confirm the setting and safety plan with a parent before going further.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to someone why the launch setting has to match where your customers are.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Anything public or online needs a real adult's approval and involvement — that's a safety judgement no tool makes for you. Keep an adult in the loop.
success_criteria: A safe, adult-approved launch setting, place, and date are decided.

lessons.title: Build the step plan and get buy-in
lessons.concept: Turn the whole launch into an ordered step plan — what the three other tracks (promote, build, sell) each need to do and by when. Then present it to your family or mentor for buy-in. Now the launch is real and the other three parts can begin.
lessons.real_example: Your plan: this week — build the product and write the promo; next week — rehearse the pitch and promote; launch day — run the stall and track sales. Everyone knows their part.
lessons.apply_task: Break the launch into ordered steps across the four tracks. Present the full plan and confirm the date.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Does your plan connect all four pillars into one launch? That's the whole two years coming together.
lessons.feedback_task: Present the plan to family/mentor and ask what you have missed or scheduled too late.
lessons.teach_back_task: Walk someone through your entire launch plan as if recruiting them to help.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Sequencing your own launch is the strategic thinking that matters here — do it yourself. An adult helps with timing and safety, since they know the real-world constraints.
success_criteria: An ordered step plan across all four tracks exists.
The plan was presented and the launch date confirmed.
